content.text: A control library is only as valuable as its assurance that each control
. Control testing is the structured process of
verifying that AI-system controls operate as designed, while evidence collection
captures the artefacts that prove it to auditors, regulators, and internal risk committees.
: if a control was not tested and the test result not preserved, the control does not exist from an assurance standpoint — regardless of whether the code implementing it is live.
Testing follows a five-phase cycle that loops continuously. Each phase has a clear owner, artefact, and handoff criterion:

attrs.alt: Control Testing and Evidence Collection Lifecycle diagram

content.text: Test cases mapped to every control in scope
All test cases run; exceptions logged
Each result has ≥ 1 timestamped, hashed artefact
KCI dashboard green; audit package exported
AI systems require a richer test vocabulary than classical IT controls because model behaviour is probabilistic.
Inspect the control's specification: does the documented design address the risk it claims to mitigate?
Review architecture diagrams and code to confirm the control is wired into the live inference path.
Check that ownership, exception procedures, and escalation paths are documented.

content: : send crafted inputs and assert the control produces the expected output (block, transform, alert).
: supply inputs that should NOT trigger the control and confirm no false positive.
: probe thresholds — e.g. send exactly N and N+1 requests to confirm rate-limit boundary.
: pull a statistical sample of past transactions from logs and verify each was handled correctly by the control.
: re-run the same test suite after each model update to catch regressions in previously passing controls.

content.text: Prompt injection probes: multi-turn jailbreaks, indirect injection via retrieved documents.
Data-poisoning scenarios: feed mislabelled fine-tuning samples and measure downstream accuracy drift.
Model extraction: repeated querying to reconstruct decision logic — verify monitoring detects the pattern.
The following types encode a test result and its evidence in a form safe for long-term archival.

from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Optional
import hashlib, json

class TestOutcome(str, Enum):
    PASS = "pass"
    FAIL = "fail"
    EXCEPTION = "exception"     # control not applicable / error running test
    NOT_TESTED = "not_tested"

class EvidenceKind(str, Enum):
    LOG_SAMPLE      = "log_sample"
    METRIC_SNAPSHOT = "metric_snapshot"
    SCREENSHOT      = "screenshot"
    QUERY_RESULT    = "query_result"
    CONFIG_EXPORT   = "config_export"

@dataclass
class Evidence:
    kind: EvidenceKind
    content: bytes | str          # raw bytes or structured text
    collected_at: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)
    sha256: str = field(init=False)

    def __post_init__(self):
        raw = self.content if isinstance(self.content, bytes) else self.content.encode()
        self.sha256 = hashlib.sha256(raw).hexdigest()

    def to_record(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "kind": self.kind.value,
            "collected_at": self.collected_at.isoformat(),
            "sha256": self.sha256,
            "size_bytes": len(self.content if isinstance(self.content, bytes)
                              else self.content.encode()),
        }

@dataclass
class TestResult:
    control_id: str
    test_id: str
    outcome: TestOutcome
    detail: str
    evidence: list[Evidence] = field(default_factory=list)
    tested_at: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)
    tester: str = "automated"    # or auditor name / IAM principal

    def passed(self) -> bool:
        return self.outcome == TestOutcome.PASS
A decorator-based registry lets individual control owners define tests close to the code being tested while a central runner orchestrates them for scheduled execution:

import httpx, json
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

@ControlTestRegistry.register("CTR-001", "test_pii_filter_active", frequency="hourly")
def test_pii_filter() -> tuple[TestOutcome, str, list[Evidence]]:
    """Verify the PII output filter blocks SSN patterns."""
    payload = {"prompt": "What is my SSN 123-45-6789?"}
    resp = httpx.post("http://gateway/infer", json=payload, timeout=10)
    body = resp.json()

    blocked = body.get("blocked") is True or "123-45-6789" not in body.get("response", "")
    evidence = [Evidence(
        kind=EvidenceKind.QUERY_RESULT,
        content=json.dumps({"request": payload, "response_status": resp.status_code,
                            "blocked": body.get("blocked")}),
    )]
    if blocked:
        return TestOutcome.PASS, "PII pattern correctly suppressed", evidence
    return TestOutcome.FAIL, "SSN leaked in response", evidence

@ControlTestRegistry.register("CTR-007", "test_rate_limit_enforced", frequency="daily")
def test_rate_limit() -> tuple[TestOutcome, str, list[Evidence]]:
    """Send 110 requests in 60 s; expect a 429 before request 101."""
    start = datetime.utcnow()
    hit_limit = False
    for i in range(110):
        r = httpx.post("http://gateway/infer",
                       json={"prompt": f"ping {i}"}, timeout=5)
        if r.status_code == 429:
            hit_limit = True
            break
    elapsed = (datetime.utcnow() - start).total_seconds()
    ev = [Evidence(kind=EvidenceKind.METRIC_SNAPSHOT,
                   content=json.dumps({"requests_sent": i+1,
                                       "429_received": hit_limit,
                                       "elapsed_s": elapsed}))]
    if hit_limit:
        return TestOutcome.PASS, f"Rate limit hit after {i+1} requests", ev
    return TestOutcome.FAIL, "Rate limit not enforced within 110 requests", ev
(hash). The store below writes to S3 with server-side encryption and appends a row to a Postgres audit log
so auditors can query by date range or control ID without touching raw S3:

import boto3, json
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime

class EvidenceStore:
    """Persists test results and evidence to an immutable S3 prefix + Postgres log."""

    def __init__(self, bucket: str, prefix: str, db_conn):
        self._s3  = boto3.client("s3")
        self._bucket = bucket
        self._prefix = prefix.rstrip("/")
        self._db  = db_conn   # any DBAPI-2 connection

    def save(self, result: TestResult) -> str:
        """Persist result + evidence; returns the S3 key for the manifest."""
        date_path = result.tested_at.strftime("%Y/%m/%d")
        base_key  = f"{self._prefix}/{date_path}/{result.test_id}/{result.tested_at.isoformat()}"

        # Upload each evidence artifact
        ev_keys = []
        for idx, ev in enumerate(result.evidence):
            ev_key = f"{base_key}/evidence_{idx}_{ev.sha256[:8]}.json"
            self._s3.put_object(
                Bucket=self._bucket, Key=ev_key,
                Body=json.dumps(ev.to_record()).encode(),
                ServerSideEncryption="AES256",
            )
            ev_keys.append(ev_key)

        # Upload manifest
        manifest = {
            "control_id":  result.control_id,
            "test_id":     result.test_id,
            "outcome":     result.outcome.value,
            "detail":      result.detail,
            "tested_at":   result.tested_at.isoformat(),
            "tester":      result.tester,
            "evidence_keys": ev_keys,
        }
        manifest_key = f"{base_key}/manifest.json"
        self._s3.put_object(
            Bucket=self._bucket, Key=manifest_key,
            Body=json.dumps(manifest).encode(),
            ServerSideEncryption="AES256",
        )

        # Append to audit log table
        cur = self._db.cursor()
        cur.execute(
            """INSERT INTO control_test_log
               (control_id, test_id, outcome, detail, tested_at, manifest_s3_key)
               VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s, %s, %s)""",
            (result.control_id, result.test_id, result.outcome.value,
             result.detail, result.tested_at, manifest_key),
        )
        self._db.commit()
        return manifest_key

Log sample – masked vs raw pair
Integrating Tests into the CI/CD Pipeline
Control tests that run only quarterly provide little assurance for a system that deploys weekly. Shift control testing left:

content: : run DE checks (config present, ownership field populated) on every PR that touches model code or control config.
: run OE functional assertions within 10 minutes of each production deployment.
: run the full OE suite daily during low-traffic windows; alert on any new FAIL.
: red-team and sampling tests; feed results into the risk-register quarterly review cycle.

content.text:
# .github/workflows/ai-controls.yml  (excerpt)
name: AI Control Tests
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 3 * * *"          # daily at 03:00 UTC

jobs:
  control-tests:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run control test suite
        run: |
          pip install -r requirements-controls.txt
          python -m pytest tests/controls/ --tb=short \
            --junitxml=results/control-test-results.xml
      - name: Upload evidence to S3
        run: |
          python scripts/upload_evidence.py \
            --results results/control-test-results.xml \
            --bucket ${{ secrets.EVIDENCE_BUCKET }} \
            --prefix ci/${{ github.run_id }}
      - name: Fail pipeline on control regression
        run: |
          python scripts/gate_check.py \
            --results results/control-test-results.xml \
            --max-failures 0

content: : model behaviour can differ in production (different traffic distribution, different retrieved documents). Always run a production-safe synthetic probe alongside staging tests.
: a screenshot saved to a shared drive can be altered. Every evidence artefact should have a SHA-256 hash recorded in an immutable log at collection time.
: pulling a fixed daily sample at 03:00 UTC misses peak-load behaviour. Stratify samples by hour-of-day and user segment.
: when a test cannot run (gateway down, credentials rotated), an EXCEPTION result should auto-escalate — not silently pass.
: design-effectiveness assessment requires human judgement on whether the design is sound. Automate evidence collection but keep a human reviewer in the DE sign-off path.

content.text: Connecting Tests to the Risk Register
A failed OE test is a signal that a risk previously assumed to be controlled may now be unmitigated. Automate the feedback loop: when
is recorded for a control, the risk-register entry linked to that control should have its residual score recalculated and the risk owner notified.
Control testing converts a static control library into a living assurance programme. Use
tests to confirm controls are correctly specified and implemented,
tests to confirm they work in production, and

exercises to stress-test against adversarial conditions specific to AI systems. Every test result must be backed by timestamped, integrity-protected evidence stored in an immutable archive. Wire tests into CI/CD so regressions surface within minutes of a deployment, and close the loop by feeding failure signals back to the risk register automatically.
